body:
🍿 IMDb.com | Full-Stack/Front-End Engineer | Seattle or Remote (US) | Full-time |  $100-$150k base (level-based) + generous $AMZN equity | [Link](https://www.amazon.jobs/en/jobs/1261725/front-end-engineer-imdb?no_int_redir=1)

> IMDb is embarking on a bold re-imagining of the consumer experience built on GraphQL, NextJS and React. We are building an entirely new distributed architecture to support iOS, Android and Web clients. We are seeking an experienced frontend engineer for the web engineering team who will work with technology, product and design leaders to deliver performant and delightful experiences that help entertainment fans explore the world of movies and shows and decide what to watch next.

> IMDb is a lot like working for a smaller company with all of the big company benefits. We know each other, we like each other, we share a passion for movies and TV. We believe in hands on career development, setting clear goals and expectations, and being transparent with opportunities to grow your career.

Hiring front-end-oriented folks to help my team migrate IMDb.com to Next.js. We're rebuilding it all, one page at a time, on Next and GraphQL, redesigning and reimagining along the way. Interested and want to chat? Drop me an email at matfrey@imdb.com and we can set up a referral.

body:
BurnerPage | Full-Stack Engineer | NYC or Remote (US only) | Full-time | $115-175k base / benefits / early equity | [Link](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1264EHpy8AKf_tar7wsFDq_hlHP9WrdTdF7A9qUbPAVg/edit?usp=sharing)

BurnerPage is a stealth startup founded by a repeat entrepreneur who sold his last company to Adobe. We are VC backed and looking to make hires 2-5 as we bring our product to market. 

The above link is for [front-end devs](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1264EHpy8AKf_tar7wsFDq_hlHP9WrdTdF7A9qUbPAVg/edit?usp=sharing), but we're looking for [backend](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1y09qDXdosWNrI8cYoOYE9V5FP6s5g3LHC-qkE99jMZs/edit) / fullstack and [machine learning engineers](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1QYJKwhTrlb5hMb_crKffPpQtiyr_plAr7aVvYhAMhqg/edit) as well (with different base pay for those positions depending on experience). BurnerPage is building the future of webpages, harnessing the power of AI and Machine Learning to automatically build webpages and tailor the viewer experience to the individual. [Note: The [current product](https://burnerpage.com/) does not yet advertise this purpose - it was just a manual prototype we launched that hit [#1 on ProductHunt](https://www.producthunt.com/posts/burner-page), but the stealth AI based product is the real focus that we're hiring for].

I'm the founder and looking to find amazing folks who love working in Next.js. Feel free to reach out to me directly to apply at avi -(a)- burnerpage.com

corise |  Full-Stack Engineer | Remote (US) | Full-time | $100-150k base | [Link](https://corise.notion.site/Full-Stack-Software-Engineer-9e7e92a5954b47eea42655ea3a220d6b)

We are a small but mighty team working on a mission to upskill the world's workforce. 

Over the next decade, 1 billion jobs will be reimagined by the development of new technologies. Companies need to reskill their employees, but they lack the know-how to do so. Individuals need to reskill throughout their careers to keep pace with changing requirements, but their learning options are insufficient.

We are building a better solution using social learning and technology to drive outcomes at scale. Our project-based learning platform empowers employees to make step function transformations in their careers and companies to upskill their workforces.

Feel free to apply using the link above.

The Bechtle web platform offers a rich B2B portfolio of IT products and services. We are a young team of out-of-the-box thinkers with a great tech stack and now looking for you/coders to help us migrate our platform to NextJs.

Besides developing with NextJs, you can contribute to a host of exciting things such as UI/UX design, headless CMS, or evaluating our Jamstack architecture. Join us as we build the future of Bechtle.

If you have any questions, please feel free to get in touch at maik.lieweries@bechtle.com, or apply directly via the link above.

We’re hiring for different engineering teams, levels and roles! Junior entries are very welcome. Please be advised that the primary language spoken across all teams is German.

We look forward to hearing from you!

**Marketforce | Senior Front End | Local | Perth, Australia | Full-time | [Link](https://marketforce.com.au/)**

Marketforce is a creative advertising agency, part of the global BBDO network. We're hiring people to strengthen the digital team. We're looking to expand our group of developers to meet the demand of our headless CMS, Next sites, and various creative digital campaign offerings. 

We're versed in: Next.JS, Typescript, GraphQL, Wordpress (as a Headless CMS), Node, AWS services.

We are a creatively lead technology team that helps transformational change, consumer engagement and better user experiences. We are a collective of specialist creative and technology experts that leverage our extensive background to help make better design decisions that enable platform capability.

Come join us in sunny West Australia, where the pandemic has hit the least. And we can still go out and hug eachother in the pub. 

Feel free to reach out to me directly, and send me a resume and some of your most recent work.

[Plex](https://www.plex.tv/) | Frontend Engineer | Remote (esp. UTC-8 - UTC+2) | Full-time | $90-160k base | [Link](https://grnh.se/25643b72teu)

Plex is hiring for a variety of positions, including a frontend engineer to work on web properties like [watch.plex.tv](https://watch.plex.tv/) and [www.plex.tv](https://www.plex.tv/). We recently published [a detailed blog](https://medium.com/plexlabs/plexs-2021-journey-with-next-js-and-vercel-c602851478c5) about our transition to Next.JS and Vercel if you want to get a better sense of some recent/current projects. We're a passionate group of movie and TV fans who love getting to build services and experiences to improve the modern streaming world, whether it's offering our own streaming catalog, managing your own personal media library, or discovering new content.

I've been at Plex for a decade now (:scream:) and love the people I work with as well as the ways I've been able to work with different technologies and grow through different roles.

Sobefy is hiring for a variety of positions, including a frontend engineer to work on custom web applications such as business directory locators, API integrations between Shopify and third party platforms. 

We recently built a [doctor locator](https://locator.dryeyerescue.com/) for one of our clients in Next.js using DynamoDB and AWS. The ideal candidate would help maintain and update the app with new features such as adding input fields (images, text, textarea, documents) as well as help display the data on customer facing pages. 

The ideal candidate will work with us for years to come, we are flexible when it comes to schedule and workflow and we are happy to train :)

body:
[Delorean](https://www.delorean.se/) | Full-Stack Engineer | Stockholm Sweden (remote possible) | Full-time | [Link](https://jobb.delorean.se/jobs/167816-delorean-soker-fullstack-webb-utvecklare)

Delorean is a digital agency in Stockholm Sweden.
Our clients are a mix of private actors and clients from governmental procurement processes, for example we have built the offical sweden.se website for Svenska Institutet.

We're currently looking for several developers such as .NET (Umbraco), Wordpress, React, Node fullstack (Strapi etc).
Many of our projects use Next.js and it is usually our go-to tool for any project of significant size.

I'm one of the company's CTO:s and have been here since 2009 (time flies!) so I'd be happy to answer any questions you have about us/possible work here.
Either contact me directly or use the link to apply. We are currently putting up more ads to cover several roles, so even if you don't see yourself 100% described in the ad (no .NET experience - no problem), consider applying anyway!

If you don't speak Swedish, contact me to know more.
We are primarily looking for people located in Sweden, however we are still open to applications outside Sweden as well. We currently have employees in the UK for example. 

I'm reachable at kalle@delorean.se

body:
Mawla | React Engineer | EU (remote possible) | Contract

Mawla are looking to grow our pool of frontend freelancers. We are always busy and having a network of strong freelancers means we know who to turn to when the next project comes up.
We have a boilerplate engine we use for all our next generation websites built on Tailwind, Typescript, Nextjs, Sanity.
A lot of the sanity and next work is done already by using the boilerplates so we're mostly looking for people with strong react, tailwind, typescript exp.

Rates, communication quality, timezones and availability are important to us so please DM me following this format:
-  Rates in $
-  English fluency - Yes/no
-  Timezone - we can't work with India and similar timezones right now.
-  Availability - full, part time, X hrs per week

We've three potential projects right now:

-  One react web app wizard for creating quotes
-  One SaaS company marketing website
-  One agency marketing website

We've met some of our favourite people in this slack so I'm enthusiastic we'll meet more great people.
PS our site is under construction newer one here - https://mawla-marketing.vercel.app/mawla-engine

projects@mawla.ie

body:
### [LaFabrica](https://www.lafabrica.work/) | Full-Stack Engineers | Remote (Worldwide) | Full-time & Part-Time | [Link](https://www.lafabrica.work/developers/register)

We are looking for Fullstack Engineers to join our platform, [LaFabrica](https://www.lafabrica.work/), a crypto-driven cooperative for Software Engineers.

**The platform...**
1. Connects developers across the world directly to remote software projects paid in US Dollars 💵
2. Offers members achievement-based dividends, distributed using our very own cryptocurrency 🏦
3. Provides startups and dev agencies dedicated, thoroughly vetted, ready-to-work remote engineering teams and individual developer resources within a range of budgets 👩‍💻👨‍💻

Our Beta pool is filling up quickly, so if you’re interested, sign up soon!

Registration: ➡️ [here](https://www.lafabrica.work/developers/register) ⬅️

**Our Stack:**
Completely flexible -- from full stack Javascript to GoLang, we encourage devs from any stack to apply.

The current platform is built out using:
* Typescript
* NextJS | React | Material UI
* NestJS | Firebase
* AWS Lambda | Google Cloud Functions

Feel free to apply using the link above or reach out to me directly.
author: spencermarx

body:
Genie AI | React Developer | London OR fully remote (UK) | Full-time | [https://apply.workable.com/genie-ai/j/8166DCED3C/](url)

Genie AI is a deep learning based software company with a mission to open source the law.

We are disrupting the legal industry by changing the business model ground up.

What Github did with open source code, Instagram and TikTok with entertainment, Airbnb with hospitality, and Uber with travel, Genie is doing with Legal contracts.

We are building a community-based AI law platform.



Requirements
About the role
We're looking for an experienced Senior React developer who has a passion for building an elegant & modern interface to solve complex real-world problems.

You are a product-focused, design thinking and curious developer who wants to turn user needs into simple yet clever solutions
You are excited to develop a community-based platform web application in React
You are interested in creating a rich, beautiful and elegant user interface to perfection
You enjoy and find it fun to build re-usable components
You are keen to protect your code with solid tests ranging from unit to automation
You care to collaborate and participate in solution design and performing experiments with the team
You are not afraid to take risks and learn advanced topics
You are eager for friendly, coherent spoken and written communication to exchange complex ideas
You build coding standards and peer review code to ensure code is neat and clean
What we need
Experience in building SaaS web-based products using;

Solid knowledge of React or NextJS framework
Typescript and Javascript
Proficient in HTML, CSS
What we would like
GraphQL
Knowledge of Design Patterns in Web development
Experience with test frameworks for unit, integration, and automation testing

What would be nice

Working with real-time collaboration applications & browser based editors
Experience with server-side rendering, web workers & service workers
Built apps with Micro-services architecture
Solid experience in responsive UI development

About our stack
Micro-services architecture, React, Next.js, Angular 12, Playwright, NodeJS, RabbitMQ, GraphQL, Redis, MongoDB, Python, AWS, Docker, Kubernetes, TensorFlow, PyTorch, ProseMirror. You're not expected to know all of this, but you will get the opportunity to learn based on your interest!


Benefits

What do Genies get?
Substantial equipment budget for you to set up a home working environment, including a top of the range laptop, monitor, wireless keyboard, mouse, and office chair
Generous stock options - we want everyone to share in our success!
Unlimited budget for business, law, or technology books
Unlimited holiday
Fully flexible working
Cycle to work scheme
Regular meditation and wellbeing sessions
Employee benefits platform where you can buy plants, food deliveries, yoga, wellbeing sessions, and more on us!
Optional pre-tax charity giving
Regular team lunches and dinners after lockdown (and plenty of social activity in the meantime!)
The opportunity to join a bright, fast-growing startup at a grassroots level and genuinely make a difference

author: simontong

body:
I don't believe it is an actual bug, but more of a feature request, since you are describing some functionality that just does not exist as of today. (Unless we document it otherwise. Can you confirm?).

From the source code, it looks like we only look for changes in the `pages` directory in dev mode: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/blob/a5bfc1eecc34e176f9f27955fde7b16e17de3c5e/packages/next/server/dev/next-dev-server.ts#L254

If you actually mean adding files when running in production (eg `next start`), that's expected behavior. See the docs:

https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/static-file-serving

>  Only assets that are in the `public` directory at [build time](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/cli#build) will be served by Next.js. Files added at runtime won't be available. We recommend using a third-party service like [AWS S3](https://aws.amazon.com/s3/) for persistent file storage.
author: balazsorban44

body:
The problem is that I place a file in `./public/uploads/myimage.jpg` while the production server is running and when I access it via `https://website.com/_next/image?url=/uploads/myimage.jpg` it says "The requested resource isn't a valid image.". 

If I restart the server (in production) then and refresh the same URL then the image appears as expected.

Basically the way it is right now I would need to restart the server every time someone uploads an image in order to display said image using the <Image/> nextjs component
author: simontong

body: See the docs link from my previous comment.
author: balazsorban44

body: You could easily achieve this by a script that toggles the name of a `.babelrc` file since that is what Next.js detects.

body:
Tailwind doesn't involve your site after build time. It compiles the optimized css and it ends there.

SSR is slowing down your site. It makes requests to CMS/API to get the page content on every request.

1. You could opt for [Automatic Static Optimization](https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/automatic-static-optimization). If contents update after build, you could opt for [Incremental Static Generation](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching).
2. Your homepage props contains unused data: `termsOfUse` and `privacyPolicy`.
author: wwwdepot

body: Thank you!
author: annaczinege

body:
I'm incredibly excited to share that Next.js 12.1 was just released, which includes **on-demand Incremental Static Regeneration**! We know many of you have been looking forward to this for a while, including myself, and I'm so happy it is here. 

- [Learn about Next.js 12.1](https://nextjs.org/blog/next-12-1)
- [Watch a demo of on-demand ISR](https://youtu.be/BGexHR1tuOA)
- [View another demo and give us your feedback](https://on-demand-isr.vercel.app/)
- [Read the documentation](https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching/incremental-static-regeneration#on-demand-revalidation-beta)
author: leerob

body:
it depends, the `schema` or any `custom folder` that is excluded from the `pages`/`app` directory won't be directly included in the client side bundle (exposed to the public). 
If the only import is in an `api route ` the schema content won't be exposed, however if a component in a page directory (example pages/home) imports the schema file then the content will be exposed.
author: briowill

body:

author: wsdo

body:
Hey folks, wanted to swing back here with an update. After digging through many different issues and discussions, I've [**made a new page in the documentation**](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/configuring/content-security-policy) ([PR](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/pull/54601)) specifically for Content Security Policy and nonces. This docs page: 

- Explains how to generate a `nonce` with Middleware
- Shows how to consume the `nonce` in a route with `headers()`
- Shows a complete CSP without needing to use any `unsafe`
- Shows how to ignore the `nonce` Middleware from running on prefetches / static assets

Further, we've patched some bugs and made improvements to `nonce` handling in Next.js that will be available in the latest `canary` version (for those of you time traveling from the future, upgrade to Next.js 13.5). We also updated the `with-strict-csp` example in the `examples/` folder, which is backlinked from the [new documentation page](https://nextjs.org/docs/app/building-your-application/configuring/content-security-policy).

Really hope this helps out, thank you all 🙏 I'll be closing this discussion out. To continue the discussion, [please go here](https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/54907).

author: Vanals

body:
Using this very simple code:

```js
// pages/_app.jx
import Head from "next/head"

export default function MyApp({ Component, pageProps }) {
  return (
    <>
      <Head>
        <title key="title">TITLE</title>
        <meta key="description" name="description" content="DESCRIPTION" />
      </Head>
      <Component {...pageProps} />
    </>
  )
}
```

And running `next build && next export` produces an HTML containing the correct elements in `head`:

```js
// out/index.html
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>

<head>
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width" />
  <meta charSet="utf-8" />
  <title>TITLE</title>
  <meta name="description" content="DESCRIPTION" />
// ...
```

The problem seems to be specific for your use case so I converted it to a discussion.
author: balazsorban44

body:
**Hi @balazsorban44  I managed to reproduce the issue.**
Could you please check the repo I created?
https://github.com/Vanals/Hello-NextJS

---
1) yarn

2) yarn next build && yarn next export

Outcome:
Meta tags from page2/index.tsx, _app.jsx and _document are in the static files (index.html & page2.html)

---

If you now go to `_document` and uncomment `getInitialProps()` and re run the step2 only the meta tags in _document will be rendered.

 ```
   static async getInitialProps(ctx) {
     const {html} = await ctx.renderPage();
     return {
       html,
     };
   }
 ```

We are currently using getInitialProps, and therefore renderPage(), in our repo to make a script work and we are also using a css-in-js library.

Is this a bug? some bad practice we are applying?

I would expect in both cases(commenting getInitialProps or not) to have all the meta tags, nested in `<Head>`, statically rendered.
As I understood from the docs getInitialProps does static rendering when exporting with `yarn next build && yarn next export`
Thank you
author: Vanals

body:
Unfortunately, I still find the reproduction a bit hard to follow. Ideally, you would cut out any code from there that's unnecessary to reproduce the actual problem, and create a step-by-step way to reproduce the problem here in the description.

I also looked up our docs and:

https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/static-html-export#getinitialprops

> getInitialProps will be called on every client-side navigation, if you'd like to only fetch data at build-time, switch to getStaticProps.

If you are doing `next export` (but in most cases really), the best solution might be using other data fetching methods instead: https://nextjs.org/docs/basic-features/data-fetching

body:
Been testing around a bit and believe the correct format is actually to put the entire slug path in a single query param named slug. So for the path

 `/company/1/Company`

the correct href value should be:

`/company/[...slug]?slug=1/Company`

@FlorisdeG 
In my case, I solve the issue like this:
```typescript
const DatasheetContainer = dynamic(
  () => import('@/DatasheetContainer'),
  {ssr: false},
) as <DataType extends Record<string, unknown>>({ prop1, prop2 }: DatasheetContainerProps<DataType>) => JSX.Element
```

and call it like this:
```typescript
<DatasheetContainer<YourDataType>
/>
```

body: Frankly I think this is a **very** important question that begs an answer from the authors of Next.js. If there is no way to do things asynchronously within the `getServerSideProps` and if everything you do in it will literally prevent the whole page from being rendered; how useful it can be?
author: scaryguy

body: @scaryguy this is a limitation of Next 12 and the only documented way to achieve partial page renders in 12 is client side fetch. Next 13 fixes the problem with Server side components and Suspense. I would recommend Next13 for this and a lot of other features: its a major advance over 12.
author: laurencefass

body: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/discussions/29117#discussioncomment-6870762
author: leerob

body:
+1, also seeing this behaviour. The documentation [here](https://nextjs.org/docs/advanced-features/dynamic-import) is underwhelming.

My guess is that with `{ ssr: true }`, your getting a smaller chunk for loading in parallel, but it behaves as if it's in the main code path.

`{ ssr: false }` is the behaviour that I'd expect from `next/dynamic` where chunks are requested as they're inserted into the dom
author: zyqxd

body:
I think there's a fundamental misunderstanding of what's going on here.

First of all, dynamic (from next) and lazy (from React), under the hood are doing link prefetch and preload.

Here's a verbose break down of these two, from Webpack's perspective: https://webpack.js.org/guides/code-splitting/#prefetchingpreloading-modules.

What you are describing is the ability, to lazy load these scripts, when the viewport is nearby, like it's done with images for example. That's out of the responsibility boundary of dynamic imports. 

The idea of the lazy loading technique is to break apart a large bundle, into chunks that are loaded as needed by the document. The fact that the main bundle is broken into chunks, means less joint time is spent parsing and rendering the first page, and gives room for interactivity. When the chunks are loaded these are again parsed and rendering into the browser is done, at a later time.

Chunking is not as simple as breaking into dynamic modules, sometimes it can hurt more than it helps. Dynamic modules have overhead, and in total, more time is spend parsing a main bundle with a bunch of chunks, compared to a single monolith bundle, the advantage is that there's breathing room in between the main bundle and chunk loading (this is a fair assumption).

You can do this by implementing two event listeners for scroll and resize, and calculate whether or not a placeholder is on the viewport as the events fire, as soon as you come near the viewport, let react know to render the dynamic/lazy component, only then the chunk would be required. The https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/API/Intersection_Observer_API, also helps out on this regard.
author: icyJoseph
icyJoseph

body:
Because Next.js is doing source code transformation during build-time you really have to be careful about how you write and structure the code.

My guess is that when a dynamic component appears directly (unconditionally) in the JSX the bundle is loaded immediately irrespective of the runtime logic.

I've made an example of getting the code structure right so it lazy-loads properly: https://github.com/Fractal-Web/next-ssr-hydration
author: MurkyMeow

body:
Hi there @cdaringe! I spoke with the team about this a bit and unfortunately its not something we are able to prioritize internally, though as always we would, of course, welcome contributions if anyone is interested.

This would be very difficult to accomplish and have many edge cases, and we are currently heavily invested in a couple of projects that we think will solve the same root problem in a more efficient manner -- namely, some improvements to routing within next.js (we are planning to put out an RFC with more detail here in the next few weeks), and react server component support.

Sorry I didn't have a more positive answer for you here and I hope that over the next few months we can bring you some new features that will greatly alleviate this pain for you all 😀
author: jescalan

body:
Hi @jescalan,

Thanks for writing.

Yes, we'd be interested in working on it. If someone could help enumerate the cases in play and confirm that such patches would be welcomed, we could budget time to work on this. We haven't seen any technical discussion on this matter yet, other than "use getServerSideProps" in an email, which does not consider or address our usage of getInitialProps discussed above. We think a config option could allow opt-in participation in next's pre-existing DCE & tree shaking, along with what is already done for the other data fetching methods.

I also recall that internally, next.js only applies optimizations to source in the `pages/*` dir, but our pages actually import page components from something akin to `@our-scope/my-page`, then re-export the components from library space. We do this because as we host multiple applications that _re-use_ page components. Thus, some consideration/support on getting tree-shaking to work more flexibly than just on source files matching `pages/*` would be helpful as well. This topic applies even if we were using `getServerSideProps` vs `getInitialProps`.

Thx for the consideration
author: cdaringe

body: Of course! I think it sounds like we should sync up and come to terms with the use case here and what's needed. I'll make sure that we get a call set up and can have this cleared up 😁
author: jescalan

body:
@balazsorban44 I was wondering whether this should be a discussion or a bug. The import path work for JS files when you override file-loader, but doesn't work when you refer the exact same path in CSS files. Also, I couldn't really find a way to refer static files in CSS (with file-loader overrides). I didn't receive any answers for this discussion as well. So I feel it should be a bug if there is really no way to achieve this.

What do you think? Let me know if a reproduction in codesandbox / some other platform would be helpful.

author: sami616

body:
Found the problem, the middleware is executed via edge functions. And edge functions don't support native modules from NodeJs. 

Reference: [edge-runtime](https://nextjs.org/docs/api-reference/edge-runtime#unsupported-apis)
author: pedroramon
